front: Is entrapment a Fourth Amendment suppression remedy or a substantive defense?
back: A substantive defense to criminal liability — it bars conviction; it does not suppress evidence.

front: What test does federal law apply for entrapment?
back: The subjective test — it turns on the defendant's predisposition to commit the crime, not merely on the fact of government inducement.

front: In the federal entrapment analysis, the controlling fact is the defendant's {{c1::predisposition}} — inducement alone is never entrapment.
back: predisposition (whether the defendant was ready and willing before the government appeared); inducement alone is never entrapment.

front: Entrapment doctrine: what may the government do versus what may it not do regarding a defendant's criminal conduct?
back: The government may furnish an opportunity to break the law, but it may not implant the criminal design in the mind of an otherwise-unwilling person and then lure that person into committing the offense.

front: Once the defense raises inducement in a federal entrapment case, what must the government prove and to what standard?
back: The government must prove beyond a reasonable doubt that the defendant was predisposed to commit the crime.

front: What did Sorrells v. United States (1932) establish about entrapment?
back: It recognized entrapment as a valid defense, arising when government officials implant the criminal design in a person who had no previous disposition and then lure that otherwise-innocent person into committing the offense.

front: Which 1932 Supreme Court case first recognized entrapment as a defense in federal law?
back: Sorrells v. United States, 287 U.S. 435 (1932).

front: What did Sherman v. United States (1958) hold about entrapment?
back: Entrapment was established as a matter of law: the government's informant implanted the criminal design in an otherwise-unwilling person and induced the crime; the defendant's lack of predisposition was clear.

front: In which entrapment case was a recovering addict pressured into drug crimes by a fellow patient acting as a government informant?
back: Sherman v. United States (1958) — entrapment was established as a matter of law because the unwilling defendant was induced by the informant.

front: What did United States v. Russell (1973) hold about supplying a legal-but-scarce ingredient to a predisposed defendant?
back: No entrapment — even though a government agent supplied a difficult-to-obtain but legal ingredient, the predisposed defendant had no defense.

front: In United States v. Russell (1973), what did the Court do with the proposed objective entrapment test?
back: It reaffirmed the subjective predisposition test and rejected the proposed objective test.

front: In which entrapment case did a government agent supply a hard-to-obtain but legal ingredient for manufacturing drugs, yet the predisposed defendant lost?
back: United States v. Russell (1973).

front: What did Jacobson v. United States (1992) hold when the government induces the crime?
back: The government must prove the defendant was predisposed independent of, and prior to, the government's inducement; 26 months of solicitation that itself created the predisposition meant the prosecution failed as a matter of law.

front: What is the Jacobson v. United States timing rule for predisposition?
back: Predisposition must exist before the government's contact; government conduct cannot manufacture the very predisposition it then points to.

front: How did Jacobson v. United States describe how settled the prior-predisposition requirement is?
back: "[T]he proposition that the accused must be predisposed prior to contact with law enforcement officers is so firmly established that the Government conceded the point at oral argument."

front: In which entrapment case did roughly 26 months of government solicitation that itself created the predisposition defeat the prosecution as a matter of law?
back: Jacobson v. United States (1992).

front: Under Mathews v. United States (1988), may a defendant deny an element of the crime and still get an entrapment instruction?
back: Yes — even while denying one or more elements, the defendant is entitled to an entrapment instruction whenever sufficient evidence could let a reasonable jury find entrapment.

front: What is the verbatim holding of Mathews v. United States (1988) on denying the crime while claiming entrapment?
back: "[E]ven if the defendant denies one or more elements of the crime, he is entitled to an entrapment instruction whenever there is sufficient evidence from which a reasonable jury could find entrapment."

front: Which Supreme Court case holds that a defendant need not admit the acts in order to raise entrapment?
back: Mathews v. United States (1988).

front: What did Hampton v. United States (1976) hold about a predisposed defendant who sold government-supplied contraband?
back: Neither the entrapment defense nor the Due Process Clause bars conviction of a predisposed defendant who sold government-supplied contraband; conceded predisposition defeated the defense.

front: In which case did a government agent supply the very heroin the defendant then sold, yet his conceded predisposition defeated both entrapment and a due-process challenge?
back: Hampton v. United States (1976).

front: Distinguish what the government supplied in Russell versus Hampton, and why neither defendant won the entrapment defense.
back: Russell: a legal-but-scarce ingredient. Hampton: the contraband (heroin) itself. In both, the defendant was predisposed, so furnishing the means did not establish entrapment.

front: Does furnishing the means or contraband to a predisposed person establish entrapment under federal law?
back: No — supplying a legal-but-scarce ingredient (Russell) or even the contraband itself (Hampton) does not establish entrapment of a predisposed person and raised no due-process bar on those facts.

front: Distinguish the subjective entrapment test from the objective test, and which governs in federal court.
back: Subjective test: focuses on this defendant's predisposition — controls in federal court. Objective test: asks whether the police conduct would induce a hypothetical law-abiding person — a non-federal/state alternative, illustrative only, that does not govern federally.

front: The objective entrapment test (would the conduct induce a hypothetical law-abiding person?) is a {{c1::non-federal / state}} alternative — it does NOT govern in federal court.
back: non-federal / state (minority) alternative; federal courts apply the subjective predisposition test instead.

front: Common pitfall: do persuasion, opportunity, or even repeated requests automatically entrap a defendant?
back: No — those do not entrap a predisposed person. Predisposition controls; treating inducement as automatic entrapment is a mistake.

front: Common pitfall: an instructor asks "would this tactic induce an average person?" in a federal entrapment case — what is wrong with that?
back: That is the state/objective framing. Federal law asks about this defendant's predisposition, not the effect on a hypothetical average person.

front: Common pitfall: does a successful entrapment claim suppress evidence?
back: No — entrapment does not suppress evidence. It is a defense to liability decided by the jury (or, when clear, as a matter of law).

front: In the entrapment decision sequence: if there was government inducement but the defendant was predisposed before that inducement, what is the result?
back: No defense — the defendant may be convicted even though induced, because predisposition existed before the government's contact.
